deceased_and_policy_information.residence_at_death: 7428 Maple Ridge Drive, Apt 304, Kitchener, ON N2E 3X9
deceased_and_policy_information.place_of_death: Grand River Hospital, 835 King St W, Kitchener, ON N2G 1G3

cause_of_death.antecedent_causes_due_to_b: Longstanding coronary artery disease with multivessel atherosclerosis
cause_of_death.antecedent_causes_due_to_c: Type 2 diabetes mellitus and essential hypertension of >15 years’ duration
cause_of_death.other_significant_conditions: Hyperlipidemia; obesity (BMI ~31); 40-year history of cigarette smoking; mild chronic kidney disease (stage 2)
cause_of_death.interval_between_onset_and_death_a: Approximately 2 hours between onset of severe chest pain and death
cause_of_death.interval_between_onset_and_death_b: Estimated 8–10 years from diagnosis of coronary artery disease to death
cause_of_death.interval_between_onset_and_death_c: Approximately 18 years from diagnosis of type 2 diabetes and hypertension to death

work_and_illness_history.unable_to_work_from_onset: no – Mr. Harris continued to work part-time up until approximately 6 months prior to his death. His functional capacity had been gradually declining over the preceding 1–2 years due to exertional shortness of breath and intermittent angina, but he remained able to perform light office and consulting duties until late 2024.
work_and_illness_history.when_ceased_working: He formally ceased working on 2024-09-30, reducing his schedule over the prior month due to increasing fatigue and more frequent episodes of chest discomfort with minimal exertion.
work_and_illness_history.date_first_attendance_last_illness_first: 2025-03-18 – Emergency Department presentation to Grand River Hospital with sudden onset of severe central chest pain radiating to the left arm and jaw, associated with diaphoresis and shortness of breath.
work_and_illness_history.date_first_attendance_last_illness_secon: 2025-03-18 – Same date, first seen by attending cardiologist in the Coronary Care Unit following initial stabilization in the Emergency Department.

manner_of_death_and_investigations.accident_suicide_homicide_details: Natural death. No evidence or history suggestive of accident, self-harm, or foul play. The patient developed sudden, severe chest pain while at home watching television in the evening with his spouse. Emergency Medical Services were called promptly. En route to the hospital, he was noted to have ST-segment elevations on ECG consistent with an acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Despite immediate initiation of advanced cardiac life support measures following cardiac arrest in the Emergency Department, spontaneous circulation could not be sustained. The clinical course, medical history, and findings were all consistent with a natural cardiac death due to acute myocardial infarction on the background of advanced coronary artery disease.

prior_medical_treatment.other_physician_name: Dr. Priya Desai, Endocrinologist; St. Mary’s General Hospital Diabetes Clinic
prior_medical_treatment.other_physician_address: St. Mary’s General Hospital – Diabetes and Endocrinology Clinic, 911 Queen’s Blvd, Kitchener, ON N2M 1B2
prior_medical_treatment.nature_of_illness_or_injury: Chronic management of type 2 diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, and associated metabolic syndrome. Over the last three years, Dr. Desai primarily adjusted his insulin regimen and oral hypoglycemic agents, provided dietary counseling, and monitored for microvascular complications (including early diabetic nephropathy and mild non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy). She also coordinated with his primary care physician and cardiology team regarding optimization of cardiovascular risk factors, including statin therapy, ACE inhibitor use, and blood pressure targets. There were no major acute injuries; care was focused on ongoing chronic disease management and prevention of complications such as foot ulcers and worsening kidney function.
prior_medical_treatment.treatment_dates: Ongoing follow-up from approximately 2022-01-15 through 2025-02-28, typically every 3–4 months, with additional telephone follow-ups and lab review visits as needed during this period.

smoking_history.smoking_length_of_time: Approximately 40 years total. Began smoking regularly in late adolescence (around age 18) and continued at roughly one pack of cigarettes per day through his mid-50s. Over the next decade he made multiple quit attempts, at times reducing to half a pack per day or switching to lighter brands, but he never maintained complete abstinence for more than several months at a time. In the final two years of his life, he reported smoking approximately 5–10 cigarettes per day, often concentrated during periods of stress or social gatherings.

physician_certification.physician_address: Dr. Laura M. Chen, Grand River Cardiology Associates, 850 King St W, Suite 402, Kitchener, ON N2G 1E8
